lines: Welcome to tonight. Take a breath. Let it out however it wants to come out... through the mouth is fine. You're here now. That's the only thing that matters for the next few minutes.

Start by getting comfortable. Move your pillow if you need to. Adjust the blanket — pull it up to your chin or push it off your feet, whatever your body is asking for. Shift your hips, your shoulders, until something settles. There's no perfect position. Just find the one that says... okay. This will do.

Notice the places where your body touches the bed. Start at the top. The back of your head resting on the pillow... the way the pillow accepts the shape of your skull. Your shoulder blades, pressing gently against the mattress... you can feel the width of them. Your lower back, arched slightly or flat, whatever is natural. Your hips... heavy, settled, two solid points of weight. The backs of your thighs. Your calves. The heels of your feet, resting at the end of the bed.

These are your anchor points tonight. Every time your mind races — and it probably will, because that's what anxious minds do, and there's nothing wrong with that — you can come back to these points. They're physical. They're real. They're not going anywhere. The feeling of your head on the pillow. The weight of your body on the mattress. Start there, and everything else becomes a little quieter.

Let your eyes close if they haven't already. Let your jaw soften... let your teeth part slightly so there's a sliver of space between them. Drop your shoulders away from your ears... notice how far they travel. Unclench your hands... let your fingers uncurl. If you were gripping the blanket or pressing your palms together, release that. These are the places where the day stores itself in your body, and right now, we're telling it... the day is over. You are off the clock.

Once more. In... hold... and out, long and slow, all the way to empty. Good. Let your breath return to its own rhythm now. The pattern has been set. Your body will remember it even as your attention moves elsewhere.
"Cast all your anxiety on Him, because He cares for you." First Peter, chapter five, verse seven.

Cast is a strong word in the original Greek. It doesn't mean place gently. It doesn't mean consider transferring. It means throw. Hurl. Get rid of. The image is physical, almost violent in its urgency. And the reason given is not that God is powerful, though He is. The reason is simpler than that. He cares for you. This is personal, not theological. The God of the universe is personally concerned about what kept you awake last night.

Tonight, we're going to practice that casting. Not in a forced, positive-thinking kind of way. We're going to name the worries honestly, look at them clearly, and then hand them over. You may need to do this more than once. That's not failure. That is the practice itself.

What thought is loudest right now? Don't go hunting for it... it's probably already there, waiting. Maybe it's about tomorrow — a meeting, a deadline, a conversation you're dreading. Maybe it's something from the past that keeps playing on a loop, reshuffling itself into different angles but never resolving. Maybe it's a problem with no clear answer... just the formless weight of not knowing what to do.

Name it. Silently, in just a few words. "I'm worried about..." or "I can't stop thinking about..." Give it language. When you name something, it becomes a thing you can look at, instead of a thing that's looking at you.
Now, let's be honest with this thought. Here's the question that actually matters: does this need to be resolved right now? In the dark? In your bed? At this hour?

Has worry ever actually solved a problem while you lay here? Think about it. All those nights you spent rehearsing conversations... running scenarios... planning for contingencies. Did the problem get solved in the bed? Or did it get solved the next day, in the light, when you could actually do something about it?

Worry rehearses. It doesn't resolve. It plays the same track over and over, each repetition a little louder, a little more urgent, but nothing changes. And meanwhile, it steals the rest your body needs... the rest your brain needs to actually think clearly tomorrow.
Jesus said it plainly: "Do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own." Matthew six, verse thirty-four.

This is not naivety from someone who lived an easy life. This is wisdom from a man who knew betrayal, homelessness, grief, and death. He knew what trouble was. And he still said... tonight is not for carrying tomorrow's weight.

So imagine you're holding this first worry in your hands. Not metaphorically... actually feel it. Give it a shape. A weight. Maybe it's heavy like a river stone, smooth and cold. Maybe it's restless, vibrating, hard to hold still. Maybe it's sharp-edged. Notice its texture. Its temperature. How it sits in your palms. You're not analyzing it. You're just holding it, the way you'd hold something you found on the ground and were deciding what to do with.

Now, slowly... lift your hands in your imagination. Raise them up. And open them. Turn your palms over and let the worry fall. Not into nothing... not into the void... but into the hands of the One who said He cares for you. See it leave your palms. See it received. It didn't shatter. It didn't disappear. It was caught. It's being held by someone bigger.
Good. Take a breath. Feel the lightness in your empty hands.

Now... is there another? There usually is. The first worry is rarely alone. Let the next one surface. Don't chase it... just let it rise. Something smaller maybe. Or something bigger that was hiding behind the first one, waiting until you dealt with that before it showed itself.

Name it. Say it silently. Hold it in your hands. Feel its particular weight... its texture... the specific flavor of unease it carries. Every worry feels a little different. This one has its own shape.

And when you're ready... open your hands again. Lift them, and release. Let it go. It will be there in the morning if you still need it. Nothing is lost by setting it down for the night. You're not ignoring it. You're not pretending it doesn't exist. You're choosing not to carry it through the dark hours, when you can do nothing about it anyway.
One more. If there's a third thought circling... let it come. Often the third one is the real one. The deep one. The one that the others were protecting, like layers around a center.
Whatever it is, name it honestly. Even if it feels too big. Even if it feels too vague to name. "I'm afraid that..." or "I don't know how to..." or simply "this."
Hold it. Feel it fully. You're brave enough to look at it. And then... open your hands one more time. Release it into care. Into keeping. Into the hands that are bigger and steadier than yours.

You've cast three cares tonight. Maybe the hands still feel a little heavy. Maybe the worries left a residue, the way a cold stone leaves a chill in the palm. That's okay. Trust grows quietly. Not in one dramatic moment of surrender... but in these small, repeated acts. Cast, and cast again, and cast again. The muscle builds over time, and it's building now.
Now we shift. The worries have been named and released. What's left is the body. And the body, freed from the weight of carrying, wants to rest.
Bring your attention to your arms. Let them lie wherever they've settled... at your sides, on your chest, under the pillow. Just notice them.
Silently say to yourself: My right arm is heavy and warm.
My right arm is heavy and warm.

My right arm is heavy... and warm. Feel the weight of it. As though it has been filled with warm sand, dense and still. Feel the warmth spreading from your shoulder down through your bicep, through the forearm, into the wrist, through the hand, to the tips of your fingers.
Now your left arm. My left arm is heavy and warm.
My left arm is heavy and warm.
My left arm is heavy... and warm. Both arms now, sinking into the mattress. Dense. Warm. Still. Like they could melt into the bed and you wouldn't mind.
Move to your legs. My right leg is heavy and warm.
My right leg is heavy and warm.

Feel the heaviness from your hip to your knee... from your knee to your ankle... from your ankle to the tips of your toes. Like warm sand filling every part of the leg, settling by gravity, compacting gently. Your right leg is heavy and warm.
My left leg is heavy and warm.
My left leg is heavy and warm.
Both legs now. Heavy. Warm. Connected to the earth through the mattress. Grounded. Still. You couldn't lift them if you wanted to. And you don't want to.
My heartbeat is calm and steady.

My heartbeat is calm... and steady. You don't have to slow it down. You don't have to do anything to it. Just notice it. It's been beating all day without your attention. Faithful. Consistent. A rhythm you didn't choose and don't have to maintain. Calm and steady, like a pulse of quiet grace.
My breathing is calm and easy.

My breathing is calm... and easy. It moves in and out without your direction. The chest rises on its own. The chest falls on its own. You can stop managing it. You can stop managing everything, just for tonight.
My forehead is cool and smooth.

My forehead is cool... and smooth. As though someone placed a cool cloth across it. The furrow between your eyebrows smoothing out. The tension in your scalp releasing. The muscles of your face softening completely. Cool... smooth... open.
My whole body is heavy and warm and at rest.
My whole body is heavy... and warm... and at rest.
My whole body is heavy and warm and at rest.

Lord... we have cast our cares on you tonight. Not perfectly. Not with unwavering faith. But honestly. Take what we've handed over — the worry, the rehearsal, the fear of what we can't control. Hold it while we sleep. We don't know how to stop worrying entirely, but we know how to open our hands. That's what we did tonight. Meet us in the imperfect offering. Amen.

The cares have been cast. The body is warm and heavy. The night is quiet around you.
Heavy arms... heavy legs... calm heart... easy breathing... cool forehead.
Nothing to solve tonight. Nothing to carry. The morning will come on its own, and you will meet it rested.
